item.title: Pydantic Blog: The human in the loop is tired
item.description: On reward functions, dopamine, and what it actually feels like when the code starts writing itself
item.content:
My thoughts on [Pydantic Blog: The human in the loop is tired](https://pydantic.dev/articles/the-human-in-the-loop-is-tired): Pydantic Blog: The human in the loop is tired
## Commentary
- Pydantic Blog: The human in the loop is tired - Take my money for writing this post!“everything I write goes into some AI black hole. There's no person on the other side actually learning anything” - So real. The part of interacting with an agent is just in-humane. It doesn’t give any skill boost. It makes us feel detached from the world. - It feels good that an organisation which is probably at the forefront on the AI revolution, Pydantic is writing such human heart touching posts. Makes them valuable then ever.

item.title: The end of creativity
item.description: A story on AI median regression
item.content:
My thoughts on [The end of creativity](https://hugodaniel.com/posts/the-end-of-creativity/): The end of creativity
## Commentary
- Hugo Daniel Blog: The end of creativity - So true. Everyone will now want to use AI to be lazy. - Creativity is something that you don’t get from letting someone else do it, its making mistakes and breaking things. But AI has made it even harder to explore creativity in some sense. It has expanded the imagination and what we can do with the kindle of a thought but beyond that, there is no bridging of that gap of fear to action.

item.title: I love LLMs, I hate the hype
item.description: I think from this blog you may misunderestimate how absolutely giddy I am about AI. I did hacking from 2007-2014, after that my whole career has been devoted to AI. I love the progress. I’m so excited for the new LLMs, self driving cars, video generation models, and coding agents. I set up a Linux box with opencode on my local GLM-5.2 last week and wow like just saying install tmux with the geohot configuration works; the Year of the Linux Desktop is finally here!
item.content:
My thoughts on [I love LLMs, I hate the hype](https://geohot.github.io//blog/jekyll/update/2026/07/12/i-love-llms.html): I love LLMs, I hate the hype
## Commentary
- George Hotz Blog: I love LLMs, I hate the hype - This is cool. Some big people are stepping up and raising the alarm on the hype noise. - This is a great thought in here “You have to be really careful, they can increase cognitive fatigue, and all the vibe coded stuff is still slop” - Perfectly put. Developers still need to hold the leash for what gets shipped.

item.title: GKCS: Current State of AI Engineering
item.description: Enjoy the videos and music you love, upload original content, and share it all with friends, family, and the world on YouTube.
item.content:
My thoughts on [GKCS: Current State of AI Engineering](https://www.youtube.com/live/ICispFxvPIY): GKCS: Current State of AI Engineering
## Commentary
- GKCS: Current State of AI Engineering - This is really eye-opening. Engineers are not going anywhere. But surely we need to adopt. Design principles still remain vital. - Interviewing is a skill that is now taking out from the code to the high level design and the details too. This is perfect. I don’t like LeetCode solving but like talking about specific design decisions I made while making projects or working in a job. That feels human.

Writing code is just one part of it. - I liked the explanation of top p and top k and temperature. That separation of idealogy makes it so much valuable. - The explanation of reinforcement learning, self supervised learning were all great to understand and know at least.

## Commentary
- GPT Models in Claude code are better than in codex? - This is wild. The reason was hidden the codex system prompt is too good. I mean bad. It sounds cheesy, hard coded times, frontend design is a mess. Ouch! that hurted. - I had used codex extensively in the month of March to May, but then it was all limited and monthly quota got exhausted in a single prompt or I think they changed some things on free tier, not sure. But haven’t used it much since then. - I regularly use Claude code and Cursor with opencode in the mix if I need something quick and dirty scripts to get out of my way.

item.title: Why write code in 2026
item.description: A bunch of reasons to write code in 2026
item.content:
My thoughts on [Why write code in 2026](https://softwaredoug.com/blog/2026/07/09/write-code): Why write code in 2026
## Commentary
- Doug Turnbull Blog: Why write code in 2026 - In the era where people defend with “I read the code”, this blog post slows down and answers a deeper question. Why write when nobody is even reading the code. - Writing code has and will continue to give me clarity along the way. But AI has made all of that clarity to be required upfront which is a bit taxing on the brain. - Like imagine starting to work on.a feature, you start with the boilerplate code and then start to write something, then you realize oh, that is not right, we need this too, we won’t require that I think. And you get the idea, the clarity comes on the way and not staring at the agent after leaving it with a one line description of the task.

item.title: Learn to code is still worth whole
item.description: Learn to code is still worth whole

My thoughts on [Learn to code is still worth whole](https://stevekrouse.com/learn-to-code): Learn to code is still worth whole
## Commentary
- Learn to code is still worth whole - What a beautiful post. The harry potter reference just won my heart. Leviosa and harry you are a wizard, what a beautiful observation. - Programming is like that, you first complain about the nitty-grities then realize, oh go to hell, you are a programmer man! You can create however you want. Building the intuition basically and understanding beyond the code just for fun. - Fun is the part that makes any programmer addicted to it.

item.title: Computerphile: Why are tokens expensive
item.description: Computerphile: Why are tokens expensive
item.content:
My thoughts on [Computerphile: Why are tokens expensive](https://youtu.be/-0HRzXk8vlk): Computerphile: Why are tokens expensive
## Commentary
- Computerphile: Why are tokens expensive - This was a eye opening and a great explanation of how you are billed for each message. Highly recommend people to check and understand to open a new chat if you have been cramping on the same chat for ages. - Not only that becomes less expensive but makes the model a bit more clear on the things, otherwise its like finding a needle in a sand. Context bloat is a thing and so is the model’s ability to understand so many things at once. - Lesson from this, start a new chat if llm is starting to deteriorate and hallucinating. Maybe even before that. I know its tempting to keep the conversation going, the model knows. a lot of things, you feel a resistance to open a new chat and provide all the details again. but that might help you solve the problem more effectively with a fresh perspective otherwise the model will create a mess of the code.

item.title: The Primeagen on “I read the code”
item.description: The Primeagen on “I read the code”
item.content:
My thoughts on [The Primeagen on “I read the code”](https://youtu.be/k13q6ecZLrw): The Primeagen on “I read the code”
## Commentary
- The Primeagen on “I read the code” - This is solid stuff. I read the code. Its feeling like a ancient thing that used to be a myth. Reading code is ok, not like hard and fast rule to necessarily to be a developer. But when you want to prove a thing, and you have read the code, you have some confidence that no other metric or number can give you. - Vibe coding is fine, you don’t have to read the code always especially when performance or quality is not important, if getting the work done is important, which I agree are many things in the world, there vibe coding and blind developing a product or a software is fine. But not always.

item.title: Building Go-Gin: Simple over Easy
item.description: A stalled startup in 2014 became Gin: a Go web framework shaped by simple-over-easy design, a radix-tree router, and an API that still works ten years later.
item.content:
My thoughts on [Building Go-Gin: Simple over Easy](https://manualmeida.dev/articles/gin-simple-over-easy/): Building Go-Gin: Simple over Easy
## Commentary
- Manu Martínez-Almeida Blog: Building Go-Gin: Simple over Easy - Ok this is really good adivce. Building things which are simple over easy. There is a subtle difference between simple and easy. Something is simple to understand like http semantics, working with apis on postman feels easy, and python is easy to learn, but golang is a simple language to understand. There is a difference, yet very fine line is blurred for most of us. - When I say easy, I mean the effort required to do something. Python looks easy on the outside, but when dealing with intricate dict mutation and internals, the easiness is no longer the ground. Its no longer simple, it was never simple, it made it look easy. Not saying Python is a bad language, but its not something you want if you are looking for lower abstraction. - When I say simple, it means its requires very little or almost no cognition or effort to understand something or do it. When I look at a Golang codebase, it is not easy to understand it, there is a lot of code, but its simple. There is a difference. The size of things matter yes, but when everything is dead simple, it sometimes or most often is verbose. More verbosity doesn’t necessarily mean simple. Java is another example when I hear the word verbose. But what I am saying it simple is intuitive. Easy is a effort thing.

item.title: do not feel like a programmer anymore
item.description: A personal reflection on how AI is changing the feeling of programming at work.
item.content:
My thoughts on [do not feel like a programmer anymore](https://blog.kulman.sk/i-do-not-feel-like-a-programmer-anymore/): do not feel like a programmer anymore
## Commentary
- Igor Kulman’s blog: I do not feel like a programmer anymore - People are actually more brave then I thought. This is true. A very honest, open hearted blog. - Tuning prompts and reviewing code is not the same as solving a deeper problem and sitting on it enough to get the bulb glowing. The moments of costly dopamine (opposite of cheap dopamine?) is getting harder to get in coding. Writing prompts, seeing AI solve problems, and guessing the outcomes doesn’t give any motivating feelings that would move us forward. - This all cycle is so messed up in the software world. People are re-inventing how we develop software. I don’t know what is going to happen, but sooner or later, the gold old days should come back that’s my wish. Otherwise I don’t like it very much, I don’t hate it always but its a bit hard-pilled medicine to digest.

item.title: University in the AI Era
item.description: In this essay, Carson Gross thinks about the best way for the university to prepare students in the AI era.
item.content:
My thoughts on [University in the AI Era](https://htmx.org/essays/universities-and-ai/#automate-everything): University in the AI Era
## Commentary
- Carson Gross Blog: University in the AI Era - This is very grounded and honest take on AI. I have hardly seen a professor express and address the concerns about AI. Really appreciated effort. - This statement that University are in a great position to distance from AI with the physical interaction making it harder to AI access is good, but not sure till when that might hold true. If robotics sees a boom like text based LLMs we are not months away from creating a mess. - The increasing of ambitious homework is the right and exciting move. I want to see myself how would I put effort to get to the goal. Its right that professors accept students will use AI and they intentionally increase the level of things they can produce. - Homework is no longer a signal that builds on the above point. I like that, because I could see myself skimming over the homework to do something more interesting maybe? As I always have been to do programming. - Visualizations are cheap. This is a underrated feature of AI, people are not trained enough to produce good quality visualizations. Maybe its moving too fast but there should be some ground where people are trained to create these helpful visualizations of things they want to learn to form a deeper understanding of a concept. - I just love the take that hand coding is still a valiant thing and thought-after thing. Yes, it looks obsolete right now. AI agents can produce 40K lines of code in a day or more. But the human behind it needs to understand the value of those lines before judging the quality of it. Coding and programming are still valuable and not obsolete. Just that you need some edge in other domain to get the most leverage out of it. Now just knowing how to invert a bnary tree is not helping anyone, people need to understand the problem, the domain in order to apply and solve problems first.

item.description: How hiring is becoming a luck filter.
item.content:
My thoughts on [HackerRank opensourced their ATS, the results are quite inconsistent](https://danunparsed.com/p/hackerrank-open-source-ats): HackerRank opensourced their ATS, the results are quite inconsistent
## Commentary
- Dan Kinksy: HackerRank opensourced their ATS, the results are quite inconsistent - This is hilarious. Why would a company like hackerrank make such a software. I think it was vibe coded? Maybe. who knows. - But building AI-systems is not just prompt, its more about the layers above and below it. It needs to deterministic, otherwise its not called a software. ATS is a software, and making it random would break the point of the software, either call it ATS or just call it a slot machine.

item.title: The daily brief by Zerodha: A deep dive into the textile industry
item.description: And how will we find jobs for the next 100 crore workers?
item.content:
My thoughts on [The daily brief by Zerodha: A deep dive into the textile industry](https://thedailybrief.zerodha.com/p/a-deep-dive-into-the-textile-industry): The daily brief by Zerodha: A deep dive into the textile industry
## Commentary
- The daily brief by Zerodha: A deep dive into the textile industry - What a great read. It shows what the textile industry is, what the past, the current and the future looks like. - The part that india is behind in the most profitable chunk of the process is so hurting. Also its a bit concerning that we are stagnant just because nobody wants to get out of the comfort zone. Its a huge risk, I understand, it might put end or stake someones livelihood out of the normal. But does that stability stall the progress of the nation? Its a bit debatable but India has surely gotten a bit stalled on this I think. - The question of finding the next billion jobs is a bigger one. Its not easy to answer that, and predict what could come next. Does AI affect this in any way, does it make it worse or better. I don’t see any way it would make it better.

item.title: Corey Schafer: Python AsyncIO, a complete guide to asynchronous programming with visualisations
item.description: Corey Schafer: Python AsyncIO, a complete guide to asynchronous programming with visualisations
item.content:
My thoughts on [Corey Schafer: Python AsyncIO, a complete guide to asynchronous programming with visualisations](https://youtu.be/oAkLSJNr5zY): Corey Schafer: Python AsyncIO, a complete guide to asynchronous programming with visualisations
## Commentary
- Corey Schafer: Python AsyncIO, a complete guide to asynchronous programming with visualisations - Just beautiful. Amazing stuff. i didn’t knew asynchronous means to pause and do other stuff while we wait for IO. I thought it was executing things in parallel. - Now I get the things, Its still requires me to build projects to fully understand it, but the idea is clear now. Its not magic anymore. The visualisations were really well made and made everything feel understandable instantly. - I think I am going to take some time to digest this. Run loop, the tasks, the threads and the groups all need to be understood in a slow way. the person making this video made a great effort in showing what async is in python. But on the other end, I also need to put in effort to understand it deeply.

item.title: Whom does ChatGPT serve? Jonathan Pageau talks
item.description: Whom does ChatGPT serve? Jonathan Pageau talks
item.content:

## Commentary
- Whom does ChatGPT serve? Jonathan Pageau talks - A great talk! I dont know the story but it felt relatable the urge to listen to someone and act blindly in instinct like animal instinct is increasing with the usage of AI. - We need to understand what we are doing and on what motivation are we doing it. The fundamental of action is being eroded by these AI text chunking machines. - Great points by the speaker here, we humans contradict ourselves when our mind is not aligned, like he gave examples of Elon Musk saying AI as existential risk to humans initially but then creating his own AI. WOW! What a full cricle here. - Must watch to get the sentiment of this topic, its a heavy topic to grasp and cannot be comprehended here.

item.title: Parquet is more than Turbo CSV
item.description: Quicker, but also more convenient
item.content:
My thoughts on [Parquet is more than Turbo CSV](https://csvbase.com/blog/3): Parquet is more than Turbo CSV
## Commentary
- CSVBase Blog: Parquet is more than Turbo CSV - This was so cool. Last week I. read about the duckdb and its engine. I was intrigued by the design of duckdb like parquet. And this week this answers that question. Seems like I am sync with the universe, its giving me hints. - So, parquet is column based storage but also row based chunking. So streaming is not possible if you need intermediate data. Since its broken down into columns, its not possible to stream half of it, either you get full or nothing. The metadata thing is so cool. it has sorted the different types of data, encodings, null and even time stamps and zones (god has anyone said time? what time). having rich metadata is not a tradeoff its a design that makes parquet really great for large data storage. - It reduces the size to almost 50% from csv that is just bonkers. Imagine what it would be from json to parquet that I did and felt for aircraft flight observatory project a couple of months back. Its quite evident, parquet is a great format for storage.

item.title: Wagtail as Django Admin on Steroids

My thoughts on [Wagtail as Django Admin on Steroids](https://timonweb.com/wagtail/wagtail-as-django-admin-on-steroids/): Wagtail as Django Admin on Steroids

Perfectly valid and genuine use case for a business to thrive. - I am thinking is there anything to build like that?

item.title: AI Loop Engineering take from Neetcode
